blocks:
Ayahs

سَبِّحِ ٱسْمَ رَبِّكَ ٱلْأَعْلَى (١)
Celebrate the Name of your Lord, the Most Exalted, (1)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ (٢)
who created and proportioned, (2)
وَٱلَّذِى قَدَّرَ فَهَدَىٰ (٣)
who determined and guided, (3)
وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَخْرَجَ ٱلْمَرْعَىٰ (٤)
who brought forth the pasture (4)
فَجَعَلَهُۥ غُثَآءً أَحْوَىٰ (٥)
and then turned it into a black scum. (5)

Content of Surah al-A'la and its Virtue

This Surah is essentially composed of two parts: one part where the discourse is addressed to the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), giving him instructions regarding the glorification of the Lord and the fulfillment of his mission, while enumerating seven attributes of Allah the Great in this regard.

The other part speaks of the humble believers and the wretched disbelievers, briefly stating the factors of felicity and wretchedness for these two groups.

At the end of the Surah, it declares that these matters are not exclusive to the Holy Quran; rather, they are truths emphasized in previous scriptures and scrolls—the scrolls of Ibrahim and Musa.

Numerous narrations have been relayed regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah:

Among them, we read in a hadith from the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Whoever recites Surah al-A'la, Allah will grant him ten good deeds for every letter that Allah revealed to Ibrahim, Musa, and Muhammad (صلوات اللَّه عليهم)." [^1]

And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "Whoever recites 'Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High' in his obligatory or recommended prayers, it will be said to him on the Day of Resurrection: 'Enter Paradise from whichever of the gates of Paradise you wish, if Allah wills!'" [^1]

It is also mentioned in several narrations that whenever the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) or the Imams of Guidance (عليه السلام) recited the verse "سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى‌", they would act upon this command and say: "Subhana Rabbi al-A'la" (Glory be to my Lord, the Most High). [^2]

In another narration, one of the companions of Ali (عليه السلام) says: "I prayed behind him for twenty nights, and he recited nothing in the prayer except Surah 'Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High.' He would say: 'If you knew what blessings are in it, every one of you would recite it ten times every day; and whoever recites it, it is as if he has recited the books and scrolls of Musa and Ibrahim.'" [^3]

In short, it can be inferred from the collection of narrations on this subject that this Surah holds a special importance, to the extent that we read in a hadith from Ali (عليه السلام): "This Surah was beloved to the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)." "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) used to love this Surah: 'Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High.'" [^4]

Regarding whether this Surah was revealed in Mecca or Medina, there is discussion among commentators. The popular view is that it was revealed in Mecca, while some believe it is Medinan.

Allamah Tabataba'i (may Allah have mercy on him) prefers that the first part of the Surah be Meccan and the end of the Surah be Medinan, because it speaks of prayer and zakat, and according to the commentary received from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), the reference is to the "Eid al-Fitr prayer" and "Zakat al-Fitra," and we know that the fasting of the holy month, the Eid prayer, and Zakat al-Fitra were revealed in Medina. [^1]

However, the possibility exists that the command for prayer and zakat in the final section of this Surah is a general command, even if the Eid al-Fitr prayer and Zakat al-Fitra are considered among its clearest instances; and we know that "interpretation by clear instance" (tafsir bi al-misdaq) is very frequent in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

Therefore, the popular view stating the entire Surah is Meccan does not seem unlikely, especially since the verses at the beginning and end of the Surah are completely harmonious in terms of their rhythmic endings (maqati'), and it is difficult to say one part was revealed in Mecca and another in Medina. It is also mentioned in a narration that every group of Muslims who entered Medina would recite this Surah for the people of Medina, [^2] and the possibility that only the beginning was recited and the end was revealed in Medina is very remote.

[^1]: "Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 533. [^1] and [^2]: "Ibid." [^3]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 5, p. 544. [^4]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 472. [^1]: "Al-Mizan," Vol. 20, p. 386. [^2]: "Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 337—this is a detailed hadith of which we have mentioned the general meaning.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

Glorify the name of your Lord, the Most High (1) Who created and proportioned (2) And Who destined and guided (3) And Who brought out the pasture (4) And then made it black stubble (5).

Commentary

Glorify Allah the Great

This Surah, which is in fact the essence of the school of the Prophets and the call of the messengers, begins with the glorification and sanctification of the Lord. At the start, addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it says: "Purify the name of your Most High Lord from every defect and deficiency" (سَبِّحِ اسْمَ رَبِّكَ الْأَعْلَى‌).

A group of commentators believe that "name" (ism) here refers to the "Named" (musamma), while another group says it refers to the name of the Lord itself—of course, the name that denotes the Named. There is not much difference between these two interpretations. In any case, the intent is that the name of Allah should not be placed in the same rank as the names of idols, and that we should sanctify His Pure Essence from any kind of defect, deficiency, attributes of created beings, accidents of body and physicality, and any kind of limitation or shortcoming—unlike the idolaters who placed His name alongside the names of their idols, or those who consider Him a body or physical.

The expression "Most High" (al-A'la) expresses the reality that He is superior and higher than anyone, anything, and whatever we can imagine, and beyond every fancy, comparison, suspicion, and whim, and every kind of manifest or hidden polytheism.

The expression "your Lord" (Rabbika) points to the fact that the Lord to whom you call the people is distinct from the "lord" of the idolaters.

After these two descriptions (Lord and Most High), He explains it by stating five other attributes, all of which are an exposition of the Supreme Lordship of the Lord. He says: "The same God Who created and proportioned" (الَّذِي خَلَقَ فَسَوَّى‌).

"Sawwa" is from the root "taswiyah," meaning to give order and to arrange. It has a broad concept that includes all the systems of the universe, whether the systems governing the galaxies and celestial stars, or those governing earthly creatures, especially man in terms of body and soul. The fact that some commentators have interpreted it only as the specific system of human limbs and eyes, or his upright stature, is in fact the mention of a limited instance of this vast concept.

In any case, the system of the world of creation—which includes everything from the largest celestial galaxies to simple subjects like the lines on human fingertips mentioned in Surah al-Qiyamah: "Yes, We are Able to proportion his fingertips" (Qiyamah: 4)—is a vivid witness to His Lordship and a proof of the existence of the Lord. Within this short expression lies a world of meaning.

After the issue of creation and the ordering of existence, He turns to the subject of planning for the movement toward perfection and the guidance of beings in this path, adding: "And Who destined and guided" (وَ الَّذِي قَدَّرَ فَهَدى‌).

The meaning of "destining" (taqdir) is the measurement and determination of the programs of movement toward the goals for which creatures were created.

The meaning of "guidance" (hidayah) is that ontological guidance (takwini) which manifests as the instincts and laws He has made dominant over every creature (including both internal and external motivations).

For example, on one hand, He created the mother's breast and its milk for the nourishment of the infant and gave the mother intense maternal affection, and on the other hand, He created an instinct in the infant that draws him toward the mother's breast. This dual readiness and attraction toward the goal is seen in all creatures.

In short: careful observation of the structure of every creature and the path it traverses throughout its life clearly shows this reality—that it has a precise plan, and a powerful hand of guidance is behind it, helping it to execute these programs. This is another sign of the Lordship of the Lord.

Of course, in the case of humans, besides the program of ontological guidance, there is another type of guidance that takes place through revelation and the sending of Prophets, called legislative guidance (tashri'i). Interestingly, the legislative guidance of man in all fields complements his ontological guidance.

A similar meaning appears in verse 50 of Surah Ta-Ha, where Musa (عليه السلام), in response to Pharaoh's question "Who is your Lord?", replies: "Our Lord is He Who gave each thing its form and then guided [it]."

The concept of this statement was known in general terms during the time of Musa ibn 'Imran or the revelation of the Quran, but today, with the progress of human sciences and knowledge in the field of recognizing types of beings—especially animals and plants—it has become clearer than ever. Thousands upon thousands of books have been written regarding this "destining" and "ontological guidance," yet they confess that what remains unsaid is far greater.

In the next stage, He refers to plants and especially the food of livestock, adding: "He Who brought out the pasture" (وَ الَّذِي أَخْرَجَ الْمَرْعى‌).

The expression "brought out" (akhraja) points to the idea that it is as if all of these existed within the heart of the earth, and Allah sent them forth. It is obvious that the nourishment of animals is a prelude to the nourishment of humans, and its benefit ultimately returns to mankind.

Then He adds: "And then He made it black stubble" (فَجَعَلَهُ غُثاءً أَحْوى‌).

"Ghutha'" originally means dry plants that float on a flood; also, the foam that appears on a boiling pot is called "ghutha'." This expression is used as a metaphor for anything that is wasted or lost in vain. In the verse under discussion, it means dried and crumbled plants.

"Ahwa" is from the root "huwah," meaning a dark green color and sometimes meaning black. Both return to one meaning, because dark green always tends toward blackness. This expression is used because dry plants, when they accumulate on top of each other, gradually tend toward blackness.

The choice of this expression, while in the context of stating divine blessings, might be due to one of three reasons:

First, this state of plants reflects the transience of the world and is always considered a lesson for humans. The same plant that was green, lush, fresh, and joy-inducing in the spring season becomes dry, lusterless, dead, and black-colored after a few months, and with its silent tongue, it expresses the instability of the world and the rapid passage of time.

Second: Dried plants, when they accumulate and rot, turn into a type of natural fertilizer that gives the earth enough power to nurture new plants.

Third: Some have said that in this verse there is an allusion to the issue of the formation of coal from plants and trees. For we know that coal, which is one of the most important sources of energy on Earth and which man has used extensively in his daily life and industries, is the remains of plants and trees that dried up and were buried in the earth millions of years ago, becoming petrified and black-colored over time.

Some scientists believe that the pastures that have turned into coal today grew about two hundred and fifty million years ago and were then buried in the heart of the earth! These pastures are so vast that if we consider the current world consumption of coal, they would meet the needs of the world's people for more than four thousand years! [^1] Of course, interpreting the verse specifically with this last meaning seems unlikely, but the verse can have a comprehensive meaning that includes all three interpretations.

[^1]: This last interpretation appears in the book "The Quran Above the Ages" written by A. Noufal, translated by Bahrampour (p. 123).

In any case, "black stubble" (dried, dark-colored plants) contains many benefits: it is suitable food for animals in winter, a means of fuel for humans, and a suitable fertilizer for the lands.

It is noteworthy that in the seven descriptions mentioned in the above verses (Lordship, Highness, Creation, Proportioning, Destining, Guidance, Growing of plants), the issue of the Supreme Lordship of the Lord is explained in the best possible way through the last five attributes. Studying them introduces man well to the station of the High Lordship of Allah, casts the light of faith into his heart, recounts the most important divine blessings in summary, and awakens the sense of gratitude in man.

Key Point

The issue of destining (measurement) and the general guidance of beings, which are counted among the manifestations of the Lordship of the Lord in the above verses, are matters that the more time passes and human science and knowledge progress, the more truths emerge from them.

Scientific discoveries allow us to see new, amazing, and more exciting aspects of this destining and guidance in all particles of the universe. Some commentators here, citing the writings of the famous scientist Cressy Morrison in the book "Man Does Not Stand Alone," have brought examples of this great secret regarding the guidance of animals and various types of living beings, a part of which is mentioned below with slight modification and completion:

  1. Migratory birds, which sometimes travel thousands of kilometers a year over oceans, forests, and deserts, never lose their nests, and after returning, they come back exactly to their original homeland. Similarly, honeybees, no matter how far they go from their hive or how much the wind blows them here and there, return exactly to their nest, whereas man needs signs and precise addresses and guides to return to his homeland.

  2. Insects have microscopic eyes whose structure and visual power amaze man, while birds of prey have telescopic eyes.

  3. To find his way at night, man must use a light source, but many birds see their targets in the ultimate gloom and darkness of the night. This is through eyes that are sensitive to infrared rays, as well as a radar-like system that has been left as a legacy in some of them.

  4. Dogs, due to having an extra sense of smell, can recognize any animal in their path through scent, whereas man, even with the tools at his disposal, is not capable of such a thing.

  5. All animals perceive sounds whose vibration intensity is beyond our hearing power, and their hearing is far more precise than ours, although man has been able to compensate for this with scientific tools and instruments, hearing the sound of a fly's wing several kilometers away as if it were on his earlobe! Perhaps this difference in power that Allah has granted between man and animal is because man is capable of compensating for his weaknesses through science and intellect, but animals are not.

  6. There is a type of small fish that lives in the sea for years, then returns to the same stream where it was born to spawn, moving against the waves. It finds its original homeland, which is suitable for its nurturing, after years at a great distance!

  7. And even more amazing than this is the story of some aquatic animals that travel this path in reverse.

Ayahs

سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلَا تَنسَىٰٓ (٦)
We shall have you recite [the Quran], then you will not forget [any of it] (6)
إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْجَهْرَ وَمَا يَخْفَىٰ (٧)
except what Allah may wish. Indeed, He knows the open and what is hidden. (7)
وَنُيَسِّرُكَ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ (٨)
And We shall ease you into facility. (8)
فَذَكِّرْ إِن نَّفَعَتِ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ (٩)
So admonish, for admonition is indeed beneficial: (9)
سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَن يَخْشَىٰ (١٠)
he who fears [God] will take admonition, (10)
وَيَتَجَنَّبُهَا ٱلْأَشْقَى (١١)
and the most wretched will shun it (11)
ٱلَّذِى يَصْلَى ٱلنَّارَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰ (١٢)
—he who will enter the Great Fire, (12)
ثُمَّ لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ (١٣)
then he will neither live in it, nor die. (13)

Commentary

We Prepare You for Every Good Deed

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the Lordship and "Oneness" (Tawhid) of the Sustainer. Following that, the verses under discussion speak of the Quran and the "Prophethood" of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. While the previous verses addressed the "general guidance" of all beings, these verses speak of the "guidance of mankind." Finally, the previous verses mentioned the glorification (Tasbih) of the Lord, the Most High, and these verses discuss the Quran, which is the manifest expression of this glorification.

He says: "We shall gradually recite to you, and you shall never forget (سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلا تَنْسى‌)."

Therefore, do not hasten during the descent of revelation, and never worry about forgetting the Divine verses. He who sent these great verses for the guidance of humanity is Himself their Protector and Guardian. Indeed, He establishes the imprint of these verses in your pure chest such that the dust of forgetfulness shall never settle upon them.

This is similar to what is mentioned in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 114: "...and do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you, and say: 'My Lord, increase me in knowledge.'" And in Surah Al-Qiyamah, verse 16, we read: "Move not your tongue with it to hasten with it. Indeed, upon Us is its collection and its recitation."

Then, to prove the power of Allah and that every good and blessing comes from Him, He adds: (You shall not forget any of the Divine verses) "Except what Allah wills; indeed, He knows the manifest and what is hidden" (إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللَّهُ إِنَّهُ يَعْلَمُ الْجَهْرَ وَ ما يَخْفى‌).

The concept of this expression is not that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله actually forgets any of the Divine verses—otherwise, confidence in his words would be undermined—rather, the goal is to state the reality that the gift of memorizing Divine verses is from Allah. Therefore, at any moment He wills, He could take it back from His Prophet. In other words, the goal is to clarify the difference between the Essential Knowledge of Allah and the bestowed knowledge of His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

This verse is actually similar to what is mentioned regarding the eternity of the people of Paradise in Surah Hud, verse 108: "And as for those who were glad, they will be in Paradise, abiding therein as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord should will—a gift without end."

It is certain that the people of Paradise will never leave Paradise, and the end of the verse itself bears witness to this. Therefore, the phrase "except what your Lord should will" refers to the sovereignty of Allah's will and power, and the connection of everything to His volition and desire, both in its initial creation and its permanence.

Among the matters that testify to this meaning is that remembering some things and forgetting others is common among all humans; this is not a distinction that Allah would mention as a special gift for His Prophet. Therefore, the intent is the preservation of all the verses of the Quran and the laws and teachings of Islam.

Some have also said that this exception refers to verses whose content and recitation were both abrogated.

However, this commentary seems very unlikely, as the existence of such verses is fundamentally unproven.

Some have also suggested that the exception pertains to the matter of recitation. Thus, the meaning of the verse would be: "We shall soon recite to you and explain Our verses, except those verses which your Lord has willed to remain concealed in His hidden knowledge." This commentary also seems unlikely given the context of the verse.

The phrase "Indeed, He knows the manifest and what is hidden" is actually an explanation of the reason for what was mentioned in the phrase "We shall recite to you." It indicates that Allah, who is aware of all manifest and hidden realities, bestows upon you through revelation whatever was and is needed by humanity, and He will neglect nothing in this regard.

There is also the possibility that the meaning is that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله should not hasten in receiving revelation and should not fear forgetfulness, because Allah, the Knower of manifest and hidden realities, has promised that he will not forget anything.

In any case, this is one of the miracles of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: that he would memorize long verses and chapters with a single recitation by Gabriel and would always remember them, never forgetting anything.

Then, He comforts the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, adding: "And We will ease you toward ease" (وَ نُيَسِّرُكَ لِلْيُسْرى‌) [1].

[1]: Some have said that the concept of the verse is actually "We will ease the easy path for you," where a transposition has occurred for emphasis to become "We will ease you toward ease." Of course, this is if "ease you" (nuyassiruka) is not taken to mean "grant you success" (nuwaffiquka); otherwise, there is no need for transposition (reflect on this).

In other words, the goal is to state the reality that on the path you have ahead, there are many difficulties and hardships—both in receiving and preserving revelation, and in conveying and fulfilling the message, as well as in performing good deeds and acting upon them. In all these matters (receiving revelation, conveying, spreading, teaching, and acting upon it), We will assist you and make the difficulties easy for you.

This phrase may also refer to the content of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call and the Divine obligations and programs; meaning its content is easy, and his Sharia is a lenient and easy Sharia, and there are no grueling or back-breaking obligations in this Divine religion.

Therefore, the above verse has a very broad meaning, even though many commentators have limited it to one of its dimensions.

Truly, if it were not for Divine help, success, and victory, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله triumph over all those difficulties would have been impossible.

The life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself was a perfect example of teaching this reality. He was not strict in anything—whether in clothing, food, mount, or living essentials. He would eat any suitable food, wear any kind of clothing that was not offensive, sometimes rest on a bed, sometimes on a rug, and sometimes even on the desert sands, and he was free from every kind of attachment or formality.

After mentioning the gift of heavenly revelation to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the promise of success and the easing of affairs for him, He proceeds to mention his most important duty, saying: "So remind, if the reminder should benefit" (فَذَكِّرْ إِنْ نَفَعَتِ الذِّكْرى‌).

Some have said: The meaning is that reminding is beneficial in any case, and those who do not benefit from it at all are few. Furthermore, it at least serves to establish the proof (itmam al-hujjah) against the deniers, which is itself a great benefit [1]. Meanwhile, others believe the verse has an omitted part, and its meaning is: "Remind, whether it is beneficial or not"—"So remind if the reminder benefits or if it does not benefit." This is actually similar to what appears in Surah An-Nahl, verse 81: "...and He has made for you garments which protect you from the heat." In this verse, only "heat" is mentioned, and "cold" is understood by implication.

However, some commentators insist that the "conditional clause" here has a specific implication (mafhum), meaning that you should remind where it is beneficial, and where it has no benefit at all, it is not necessary.

The possibility has also been raised that "in" (if) here is not "conditional," but rather means "qad" (certainly) and is for emphasis and realization, and the meaning of the sentence is: "Remind, for indeed the reminder is beneficial and useful."

Among these four interpretations, the first seems the most appropriate of all. The practical program of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also testifies to this, as he placed no conditions or restrictions on his preaching and reminders, and he would exhort and warn everyone.

[1]: And the fact that the Quran says: "It is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them—they will not believe" (Al-Baqarah: 6) applies only to a minority of people; otherwise, the majority will inevitably be influenced by eloquent and reaching words—some more, some less, and some very little—but in any case, well-measured words usually have an effect. Therefore, the conditional clause here is of the type of a "predominant condition" which carries no negative implication.

In the following verses, He addresses the reaction of the people toward the reminder, exhortation, and warning, and divides them into two groups: He says: "He who fears [Allah] and feels responsibility will be reminded" (سَيَذَّكَّرُ مَنْ يَخْشى‌).

Indeed, unless the spirit of "fear" (khashyah) and awe, or in other words, the spirit of "truth-seeking" which is a level of piety (taqwa), exists in a person, they will not benefit from Divine exhortations and the reminders of the Prophets. Thus, at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, He considers the Quran a source of guidance for the God-fearing, saying: "A guidance for the righteous (muttaqin)."

And in the next verse, He addresses the second group, adding: "But the most wretched will avoid it" (وَ يَتَجَنَّبُهَا الْأَشْقَى‌) [1]. In some narrations, it is mentioned that Ibn Abbas says: The verse "He who fears will be reminded" was revealed concerning "Abdullah ibn Umm Maktum," that pure-hearted, truth-seeking blind man; and some have said: The verse "But the most wretched will avoid it" is about "Walid ibn Mughirah" and "Utbah ibn Rabi'ah," who were among the leaders of polytheism and disbelief [2]. Some have said that "the most wretched" (ashqa) here refers to the obstinate enemies of the truth, because people are of three groups: a group that is knowing and aware, a group that is hesitant and doubting, and a group that is obstinate. Naturally, the first and second groups benefit from reminders; the only group that does not gain positive benefit—and the only effect of the reminder for them is the establishment of proof—is the third group.

Additionally, it is understood from this verse that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله included the third group in his reminders as well, but they would distance themselves and turn their faces away.

It is noteworthy that in these two verses, "wretchedness" (shaqawah) is placed as the opposite of "fear" (khashyah), whereas normally it should be placed opposite "felicity" (sa'adah). This is because the primary root of human felicity and happiness is that very sense of responsibility and fear.

And in the next verse, He describes the fate of the latter group as such: "The one who will enter the Great Fire [of Hell] and be cast therein" (الَّذِي يَصْلَى النَّارَ الْكُبْرى‌).

"Then he will neither die therein nor live" (ثُمَّ لا يَمُوتُ فِيها وَ لا يَحْيى‌).

Meaning he neither dies so as to be at rest, nor can the state he is in be called life; rather, he constantly struggles between death and life, and this is the worst affliction and calamity for them.

Regarding what is meant by "the Great Fire" (al-nar al-kubra), a group has said it refers to the lowest level of Hell and the "lowest of the low" (asfal al-safilin). Why should it not be so, given that they were the most wretched and obstinate of people? Their punishment must also be the harshest and most terrifying punishment.

However, some have said that describing this fire as "Great" is in contrast to the "Lesser Fire," meaning the fires of this world. As it is mentioned in a Hadith that Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said:

"Indeed, this fire of yours is one part out of seventy parts of the fire of Hell; it was extinguished seventy times with water and then flared up again. If it were not for that, no human would have been able to endure it or stand near it" [1]. In the famous Supplication of Kumayl narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read regarding the comparison between the fire of this world and the hereafter: "Whereas that is an affliction and a hardship whose stay is short, whose subsistence is brief, and whose duration is fleeting."

[1]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 8, p. 288, Hadith 21.

Ayahs

قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَن تَزَكَّىٰ (١٤)
‘Felicitous is he who purifies himself, (14)
وَذَكَرَ ٱسْمَ رَبِّهِۦ فَصَلَّىٰ (١٥)
celebrates the Name of his Lord, and prays. (15)
بَلْ تُؤْثِرُونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا (١٦)
But you prefer the life of this world, (16)
وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ وَأَبْقَىٰٓ (١٧)
while the Hereafter is better and more lasting.’ (17)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَفِى ٱلصُّحُفِ ٱلْأُولَىٰ (١٨)
This is indeed in the former scriptures, (18)
صُحُفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ (١٩)
the scriptures of Abraham and Moses. (19)

Commentary

A Program Found in All Divine Books

In the preceding verses, there was a reference to the severe punishment of the obstinate disbelievers and the enemies of truth. The verses under discussion point toward the salvation of the people of faith and the factors behind this salvation. First, it states: "He has certainly succeeded who purifies himself" (قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ تَزَكَّىٰ).

"And remembers the name of his Lord and prays" (وَ ذَكَرَ اسْمَ رَبِّهِ فَصَلَّىٰ). Thus, He considers the factors of prosperity, success, victory, and salvation to be three things:

"Purification" (Tazkiyah), "Remembrance of Allah’s name," and then "Performing the prayer."

Various interpretations have been mentioned regarding what is meant by "purification":

First, it refers to cleansing the soul from polytheism (shirk), based on the context of the preceding verses, and also because the most important purification is indeed cleansing from polytheism.

Second, it refers to cleansing the heart from moral vices and performing righteous deeds, based on the verses of "Falah" (success) in the Holy Quran—including the opening verses of Surah Al-Mu'minun which link success to righteous deeds—and based on verse 9 of Surah Ash-Shams which, after mentioning piety and wickedness, states: قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاهَا: "He has succeeded who purifies it [the soul] from wickedness and ugly deeds and adorns it with the ornament of piety."

Third, it refers to "Zakat al-Fitr" on the day of Eid al-Fitr, where one must first pay the Zakat and then perform the Eid prayer, as narrated in several traditions from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [1]. This same meaning has been narrated in Sunni sources from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام [2].

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 556, Hadith 19 and 20. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 30, p. 110; Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 740.

Here, a question arises: Surah al-A'la is a Meccan Surah, and in Mecca, neither Zakat al-Fitr was ordained, nor the fasting of the month of Ramadan, nor the ceremonies of the Eid al-Fitr prayer.

In response to this question, some have said there is no obstacle to the beginning of this Surah being revealed in Mecca and its end in Medina.

There is also a strong possibility that the above interpretation is of the nature of explaining a clear instance and applying the verse to a prominent example.

Some have also considered "purification" here to mean giving financial charity (Sadaqah).

The important point is that "purification" has a broad meaning that encompasses all these concepts: cleansing the soul from the pollution of polytheism, cleansing it from base morals, cleansing one's actions from prohibitions and any form of hypocrisy, and cleansing wealth and life by giving Zakat in the way of Allah. For according to the verse: خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَ تُزَكِّيهِمْ بِها: "Take, [O Muhammad], from their wealth a charity by which you purify them and cause them increase [tazkiyah]," giving Zakat is a cause for the purity of the soul and life.

Therefore, all interpretations can be gathered within the broad meaning of the verse.

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, the mention is first of purification, then the remembrance of the Lord, and then prayer.

According to some commentators, the practical stages for a duty-bound person are three:

First, "removing corrupt beliefs from the heart," then "the presence of the knowledge of Allah and His attributes and names in the heart," and third, "engaging in service." The above verses refer to these three stages in three short sentences [1]. It is also noteworthy that He considers "prayer" a branch of "remembrance of the Lord"; this is because until one remembers Him and the light of faith shines in one's heart—

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 31, p. 147.

—one does not stand for prayer. Furthermore, the prayer that is valuable is one accompanied by His remembrance and arising from His mention. The fact that some have interpreted the remembrance of the Lord solely as "Allahu Akbar" or "Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim" said at the beginning of prayer is, in reality, an expression of its specific instances.

Then, He points to the main factor for deviating from this program of success and prosperity, adding: "But you prefer the worldly life" (بَلْ تُؤْثِرُونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا).

"While the Hereafter is better and more enduring" (وَ الْآخِرَةُ خَيْرٌ وَ أَبْقىٰ). And this is, in truth, the same matter mentioned in the hadiths: "Love of the world is the root of all sin" [1]. While no intellect allows a person to sell the "eternal abode" for "perishing goods" and to prefer these brief pleasures, mixed with all kinds of pain and suffering, over all those everlasting blessings free from any unpleasantness.

Finally, at the end of the Surah, He says: "Indeed, these instructions that have been mentioned are not limited to this Divine Book, but have also appeared in the previous scriptures and books" (إِنَّ هذا لَفِي الصُّحُفِ الْأُولىٰ).

[^1]: This hadith has been narrated with various wordings from Imam al-Sadiq and Imam al-Sajjad Ali ibn al-Husayn, and indeed from all the Prophets, which is evidence of its extraordinary importance (Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, pp. 556-557).

"The scriptures of Abraham and Moses" (صُحُفِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَ مُوسىٰ) [1]. Regarding what "this" (هذا) refers to, there are various opinions:

A group has said it refers to the recent instructions regarding "purification," "prayer," and "not preferring the worldly life over the Hereafter," because these were among the most fundamental teachings of all Prophets and appeared in all Divine Books.

Others consider it a reference to the entire "Surah," as it begins with Monotheism (Tawhid), continues with Prophethood, and ends with practical programs.

In any case, this expression shows that the highly important content of this Surah, or specifically the recent verses, are among the fundamental principles of the world of religions and the basic teachings of all Prophets and Messengers. This itself is a sign of the greatness of the Surah and the importance of these teachings.

"Suhuf" is the plural of "Sahifah," meaning here a tablet or a page upon which something is written.

The above verses show that Prophet Abraham عليه السلام and Moses عليه السلام also possessed Divine Books.

In a narration from Abu Dharr, we read that he said: I asked the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, "How many Prophets were there?"

He replied, "One hundred and twenty-four thousand."

We asked, "How many of them were Messengers (Rasul)?"

He replied, "Three hundred and thirteen, and the rest were only Prophets (Nabi)."

[^1]: It is possible that "the scriptures of Abraham and Moses" is an explanation for "the former scriptures." It is also possible that it is a clarification and a clear instance of it. In the first case, it includes all the books of previous Prophets, and in the second case, only the scriptures of Abraham and Moses.

I asked, "Was Adam a Prophet?"

He said, "Yes, Allah spoke with him and created him with His own hand." Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله added, "O Abu Dharr! Four of the Prophets were Arabs: Hud, Salih, Shu'ayb, and your Prophet."

I said, "O Messenger of Allah! How many books did Allah reveal?"

He said, "One hundred and four books: ten books to Adam, fifty books to Seth (Shith), and thirty books to Enoch (Akhnukh) who is Idris—and he was the first to write with a pen—and ten books to Abraham, and also the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Furqan (revealed to Moses, Jesus, David, and the Prophet of Islam)" [1]. The expression "the former scriptures" regarding the books of Abraham and Moses is in contrast to the recent scriptures revealed to Prophet Jesus and the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Key Points An Analysis of the Hadith "Love of the World is the Root of All Sin"

Certainly, for believers, this Quranic calculation mentioned in the above verses comparing the world to the Hereafter—stating "the Hereafter is better and more enduring"—is completely clear. Yet, despite this, how does a believer often trample upon his own knowledge and become tainted by sins and crimes?! The answer to this question is one sentence: the dominance of desires over the human being, and the source of the dominance of desires is the love of the world.

Love of the world—including love of wealth, status, sexual desire, seeking superiority, excellence,

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 476.

—self-indulgence, seeking revenge, and the like—sometimes creates such a storm in the human soul that it blows away all his knowledge, and sometimes even destroys his sense of discernment, resulting in him preferring the worldly life over the Hereafter.

The fact that in Islamic narrations the love of the world is repeatedly counted as the source of all sins is an objective and perceptible reality that we have tested many times in our own lives and the lives of others.

Therefore, to cut the roots of sin, there is no way but to remove the love and passion for the world from the heart.

We must look at the world as a means, a passage, a bridge, or a farm.

It is impossible for lovers of the world, when faced with the choice between "attaining the goods of this world and reaching the pleasure of Allah," to prefer the latter.

If we look at criminal records, we clearly see the reality of the above hadith in them.

When we consider the causes of wars, bloodshed, and killings (even among brothers and friends), the footprint of the love of the world is evident and visible in all of them.

But how can one remove the love of the world from the heart when we are all children of the world, and the child's affection for the mother is a natural thing?! This requires intellectual, cultural, and ideological training, followed by the refinement of the soul (tahdhib al-nafs).

Among the things that can provide the greatest help to the wayfarers on this path is observing the end of the affairs of the world-worshippers.

What did the Pharaohs finally do with all that power and financial resources? How much did Qarun take with him from all those treasures whose keys several strong men struggled to carry?! The great powers we see in our own age, whose book of life is overturned by a breeze, and who fall from the throne of power with one turn of day and night, leaving their palaces and wealth behind and fleeing, or being hidden under the earth—they are themselves our best preachers and teachers.

We end this extensive discussion with a hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام which is very eloquent and expressive:

He was asked, "What is the best deed in the sight of Allah?"

He replied: ما من عمل بعد معرفة اللَّه عز و جل و معرفة رسوله (ص) افضل من بغض الدنيا: "There is no deed after the knowledge of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and the knowledge of His Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله better than the hatred of the world!" Then he added: "Because for the love of the world there are many branches, and for sins there are also many branches. The first thing with which Allah was disobeyed was the sin of Iblis when he refused and 'waxed proud' and became of the disbelievers.

Then it was 'greed,' which became the source of the sin (and the leaving of the better) of Adam and Eve, at the time when Allah the Almighty said to them: 'Eat from wherever you wish in Paradise, but do not approach this (forbidden) tree, lest you be among the wrongdoers.' But they went after something they did not need! And this remained for their children until the Day of Resurrection, for most of what man seeks, he does not need (needs are often not the source of sin; what is the source of sin are whims and things in excess of need). Then it was 'envy,' which was the source of the sin of the son of Adam; he envied his brother and killed him.

And from its branches are the love of women, the love of the world [1], the love of leadership, the love of comfort, the love of talk, the love of superiority, and the love of wealth. These are seven traits that are all gathered in the love of the world, and therefore the Prophets and scholars, after becoming aware of this reality, said: 'Love of the world is the root of all sin'" [2].

O Allah! Remove the love of the world, which is the root of all sins, from our hearts. O Lord! Take our hand in the winding path of perfection and guide us to the intended destination. O God! You are aware of the hidden and the manifest; forgive our secret and open sins by Your grace and generosity.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-A'la. Beginning of the blessed month of Ramadan, 1407.

[^1]: It seems that "love of the world" here refers to "love of remaining in the world," which is considered one of the seven branches and usually appears in the form of "long hopes and far-reaching desires" (reflect on this). [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on the Love of the World and Greed for it, Hadith 8. In this same chapter of Usul al-Kafi, 17 narrations are cited in this regard which are very instructive.
